FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=7514, ages 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018), 21 recruitment sites across U.S., analyzed Years 1-3 data (5.0 release).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis; modified Poisson regression with 3-year longitudinal follow-up (Year 1 social media time predicting Year 3 alcohol sipping).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published journal article in Substance Use & Misuse (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 direct hit: Social media time (Year 1) as IV paired with alcohol sipping (Year 3) as DV. RR 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43), p<0.001. Phone/SM-specific variable (time on social media, self-reported hours) with individual-trait outcome (substance use initiation). Other side is individual-trait level (behavioral/substance use outcome, not demographic-only).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: risk ratios with 95% confidence intervals in Table 2 (Models 1-3); indirect/direct effects with bootstrapped 95% CIs in Table 3 (mediation analysis via GSEM).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric quantification throughout: RR for social media time (Year 1) on sipping (Year 3) = 1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43), p<0.001; indirect effect B=0.011 (BC 95% CI 0.007-0.016); proportion mediated 25.0% (95% CI 11.0-39.3).

FT_EC7_reason: Social media appears as IV (time on social media, Year 1), not as DV-only. Design is longitudinal but SM-as-IV analysis is present, so FT-EC7 carve-outs do not apply.
notes: Paper qualifies on the basis of a direct phone/SM-specific variable (social media time) paired with an individual-trait outcome (alcohol sipping).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=4,718, ages 10-15 years (data collection 2018-2021), analyzed from 4.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional data from Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 3 (2019-2021) ABCD Study; multivariate linear and logistic regression models examining associations between screen time/physical activity and CVD risk factors.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published journal article in BMC Public Health (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate. Methods state screen time composite includes "television shows, movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, social media, and texting." Results report only aggregate "recreational screen time" (hours per day, categorized) associated with CVD risk; no separate coefficients for social media time, texting hours, or video chat alone. Step 2 composite-content check: Composite includes TV, movies, streaming video (non-phone platform), multi-player gaming (unspecified platform) bundled with social media/texting without separate breakout in reported results. Non-phone/non-SM ingredients present and not isolated.

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because screen time composite bundles television, movies, multi-player gaming, and other non-phone/non-SM media with social media and texting without separately reporting phone/SM-specific estimates.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, N=9,538, ages 9-10 years at baseline (2016-2018), two years of follow-up to Year 2 (4.0 release), 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis; mixed-effects models analyzing associations between baseline self-reported screen time and parent-reported mental health symptoms (Child Behavior Checklist) with two-year longitudinal follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published journal article in BMC Public Health (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate isolated with its own coefficient. Methods state screen time includes "television shows, movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, texting, video chatting (e.g., Skype, Facetime), and social media (e.g., Facebook, Instagram, Twitter)." Results report associations for total screen time only (e.g., β=0.10 for depressive symptoms, β=0.07 for conduct symptoms). Text mentions "video chat, texting, videos, and video games" had greatest associations but no separate numeric estimates (no table showing per-modality coefficients). Step 2 composite-content check: Composite includes TV, movies, video streaming (non-phone device), multi-player gaming (platform unspecified), texting, video chat, and social media. Non-phone/non-SM ingredients (TV, movies, streaming, unspecified gaming) are bundled without separate breakout in reported results tables (Table 2 shows only aggregate screen time models).

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 because screen time composite includes TV, movies, video streaming, and unspecified gaming bundled with social media and texting. No separate phone/SM-specific numeric estimates are reported in any results table or supplementary material.
